threats.title: No Cap on Cumulative TGT Deposits Causes TITN Insolvency
threats.hypothesis_description: onTokenTransfer accepts unlimited TGT deposits without checking cumulative amounts against TGT_TO_EXCHANGE. If total TGT deposited exceeds 579M, the resulting totalTitnClaimable will exceed the deposited TITN_ARB (173.7M TITN), making the contract insolvent. Later claimers will face reverts on claimTitn when the contract runs out of TITN.
threats.investigation_rationale: The quoteTitn function computes output per-deposit using the ratio TITN_ARB/TGT_TO_EXCHANGE but never checks cumulative allocation. With TGT total supply of 1B, 579M+ TGT can be deposited. Any user can trigger this by depositing TGT — no special privileges needed.

proof_of_signal.pattern: onTokenTransfer calls quoteTitn and accumulates totalTitnClaimable without checking against TITN_ARB or any maximum cap

preconditions: lockedStatus != Locked and launchTime > 0
Within 360 days of launch
Sufficient TGT in circulation to exceed TGT_TO_EXCHANGE (579M TGT vs 1B total supply)

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: claimableTitnPerUser[from] += titnOut; totalTitnClaimable += titnOut; — no cap check
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: totalTitnClaimable can grow unboundedly as any amount of TGT is accepted
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No require(totalTitnClaimable + titnOut <= TITN_ARB) or require(totalTGTDeposited + amount <= TGT_TO_EXCHANGE)

analysis_questions: What is the total circulating TGT supply available for deposit?
Is there an off-chain mechanism limiting TGT deposits (e.g., TGT contract limits)?
How much TGT deposit is needed to cause first claim failure?
validation_requirements: Simulate depositing > TGT_TO_EXCHANGE worth of TGT
Verify claimTitn reverts for later users when TITN balance is insufficient
Calculate exact insolvency threshold

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Multiple users deposit TGT during the first 90 days (full rate). Total TGT deposited exceeds 579M. totalTitnClaimable exceeds 173.7M TITN. Early claimers drain the contract; late claimers get nothing — classic bank run.
prerequisites: Sufficient TGT holders willing to participate in the merge

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Up to 173.7M TITN at risk — later depositors lose all claimable tokens
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All TGT depositors who claim after TITN is exhausted
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Owner could deposit additional TITN, but this undermines fixed allocation model

threats.test_coverage_gap: No tests for cumulative deposit exceeding TGT_TO_EXCHANGE

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Observable via totalTitnClaimable exceeding TITN_ARB on-chain

threats.title: withdrawRemainingTitn Arithmetic Underflow Causes Permanent DoS of Post-Year Claims
threats.hypothesis_description: In withdrawRemainingTitn, the calculation `unclaimedTitn = remainingTitnAfter1Year - initialTotalClaimable` will underflow (revert in Solidity 0.8+) whenever the contract's TITN balance at the 360-day snapshot is less than totalTitnClaimable. This occurs naturally when TGT over-deposit (TECH-H001) inflates totalTitnClaimable beyond TITN_ARB, permanently blocking all post-year withdrawals.
threats.investigation_rationale: If TECH-H001 is exploitable, this underflow is guaranteed. The snapshot is set once by the first caller and cannot be corrected. Every subsequent call also reverts, permanently locking all remaining TITN in the contract.

proof_of_signal.pattern: uint256 unclaimedTitn = remainingTitnAfter1Year - initialTotalClaimable; — unchecked subtraction that underflows when balance < totalTitnClaimable

preconditions: totalTitnClaimable > titn.balanceOf(address(this)) at the time of first post-360-day call
Caused by TGT over-deposit (TECH-H001) or owner drain

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: remainingTitnAfter1Year = currentRemainingTitn (balance); initialTotalClaimable = totalTitnClaimable; then unclaimedTitn = remainingTitnAfter1Year - initialTotalClaimable
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: If balance < totalTitnClaimable, subtraction underflows permanently
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No check that remainingTitnAfter1Year >= initialTotalClaimable before subtraction

analysis_questions: Under what conditions does remainingTitnAfter1Year < initialTotalClaimable?
Is the revert permanent or can the snapshot be reset?
Can claimTitn be used as fallback (it's blocked after 360 days)?
validation_requirements: Simulate over-deposit scenario then call withdrawRemainingTitn after 360 days
Confirm revert due to underflow
Verify no recovery path exists for affected users

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: After TGT over-deposit inflates totalTitnClaimable beyond TITN_ARB, at 360 days the first caller triggers snapshot. remainingTitnAfter1Year (actual balance) < initialTotalClaimable (inflated total). Subtraction underflows, reverts. All users with unclaimed TITN lose access permanently.
prerequisites: TECH-H001 conditions met OR owner has withdrawn TITN

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: All unclaimed TITN at 360-day mark permanently locked
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All users who did not claim via claimTitn before 360 days
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Only owner can recover via withdraw() — users cannot

threats.test_coverage_gap: No edge case tests for underflow in proportional share calculation

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by comparing totalTitnClaimable vs titn.balanceOf

threats.title: Cross-Chain Dust Bridging Griefing Permanently Locks Victim Transfer Capability on Base
threats.hypothesis_description: An attacker can bridge a dust amount (e.g., 1 wei) of TITN to any target address on Base (or any non-Arbitrum chain). The _credit function permanently sets isBridgedTokenHolder[victim] = true with no reversal mechanism. When isBridgedTokensTransferLocked is true, the victim cannot transfer ANY of their TITN — including tokens they owned before being tainted — except to the transferAllowedContract or lzEndpoint.
threats.investigation_rationale: isBridgedTokenHolder is a monotonic flag (only false→true, never cleared). Any address receiving bridged tokens is permanently flagged. _validateTransfer restricts ALL transfers from flagged addresses when lock is active, not just the bridged amount.

proof_of_signal.pattern: isBridgedTokenHolder[_to] = true; — permanent flag set in _credit with no clearing function anywhere in contract

preconditions: isBridgedTokensTransferLocked == true on the destination chain
Victim address is not owner and not transferAllowedContract
Attacker has any amount of TITN on a source chain to bridge

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Permanent flag set in _credit; _validateTransfer checks isBridgedTokenHolder[from] to restrict ALL transfers
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Flag applies to ALL user tokens, not just the bridged dust amount; no admin function to clear the flag
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No clearBridgedTokenHolder function, no admin override per-address

analysis_questions: Can the owner clear isBridgedTokenHolder for a victim?
Is setBridgedTokenTransferLocked the only mitigation (global, affects all users)?
What is the minimum cost to execute this griefing attack?
validation_requirements: Bridge 1 wei of TITN to target address on Base
Verify target can no longer transfer their existing TITN
Confirm no clearing mechanism exists in the contract

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker holds TITN on Arbitrum. Calls OFT.send() with 1 wei to victim's address on Base. _credit mints 1 wei and sets isBridgedTokenHolder[victim] = true. Victim, who may hold significant TITN on Base, can now only transfer to transferAllowedContract or lzEndpoint. Their tokens are effectively frozen.
prerequisites: Attacker has any TITN on a source chain
LayerZero bridge fee for dust amount

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Victim's entire TITN balance becomes non-transferable on the affected chain
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Any TITN holder can be targeted on non-Arbitrum chains
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Only owner can mitigate by setting isBridgedTokensTransferLocked = false (global, affects all users)

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for griefing via dust bridging to non-bridged holders

threats.title: Off-by-One Boundary at Exactly 360 Days Allows TGT Deposit for Zero TITN Output
threats.hypothesis_description: At exactly block.timestamp - launchTime == 360 days, onTokenTransfer allows the deposit (uses strict > 360 days check), but quoteTitn returns 0 (uses < 360 days in both rate branches). The user's TGT is transferred into the contract but 0 claimable TITN is credited. Additionally, claimTitn reverts at >= 360 days, so the user cannot reclaim anything. The TGT is permanently locked with no TITN received.
threats.investigation_rationale: Inconsistent boundary checks between onTokenTransfer (> 360 days revert), claimTitn (>= 360 days revert), and quoteTitn (< 90 days, < 360 days branches, else 0) create a gap at exactly 360 days where deposit succeeds but output is zero.

proof_of_signal.pattern: onTokenTransfer: (block.timestamp - launchTime > 360 days) revert; quoteTitn: if(timeSinceLaunch < 90 days)...else if(timeSinceLaunch < 360 days)...else titnAmount=0; claimTitn: (block.timestamp - launchTime >= 360 days) revert

preconditions: block.timestamp - launchTime == exactly 360 days
User deposits TGT via transferAndCall at this exact moment
lockedStatus != Locked

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: onTokenTransfer uses > 360 days; quoteTitn uses < 360 days; discrepancy allows deposit with 0 output
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: No check for titnOut == 0 after quoteTitn; user TGT is accepted but zero TITN credited

analysis_questions: At exactly 360 days, which code path in quoteTitn is taken?
Is there any recovery mechanism for TGT deposited with 0 TITN output?
Can this be triggered across multiple blocks at the boundary?
validation_requirements: Set block.timestamp to exactly launchTime + 360 days
Call onTokenTransfer and verify it succeeds
Verify quoteTitn returns 0 and claimTitn reverts

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: User deposits TGT at block where timestamp equals launchTime + 360 days. onTokenTransfer passes all checks. quoteTitn falls into the else branch (timeSinceLaunch not < 360 days) and returns 0. User's claimableTitnPerUser gets 0 added. TGT is locked in contract with no TITN return and no way to recover.
prerequisites: Transaction lands in block where timestamp == launchTime + 360 days

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: 100% loss of deposited TGT for affected transaction
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Any user depositing at the exact 360-day boundary
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: None — no mechanism to return TGT to depositor

threats.test_coverage_gap: No boundary testing at exactly 360 days timestamp

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by checking quoteTitn output before depositing

threats.title: lzEndpoint Transfer Exemption Enables Permanent Token Loss via Direct Transfer
threats.hypothesis_description: The _validateTransfer function exempts transfers to lzEndpoint (to != lzEndpoint condition). On Arbitrum where all transfers are restricted, users may transfer TITN directly to the LayerZero endpoint address, thinking it will bridge their tokens. However, actual OFT bridging uses _burn internally via OFT.send(), not transfer. Tokens sent via direct transfer to the endpoint are permanently lost.
threats.investigation_rationale: OFT V2's _debit function calls _burn, which bypasses transfer/transferFrom entirely. The to != lzEndpoint exemption serves no functional purpose for legitimate bridging and only creates a vector for permanent token loss. This is dead/misleading code that enables harmful user actions.

proof_of_signal.pattern: to != lzEndpoint — exemption allows transfers TO the LZ endpoint address, but OFT bridging uses _burn (not transfer)

preconditions: User is on Arbitrum (or any chain with transfer restrictions active)
User calls transfer(lzEndpoint, amount) instead of OFT.send()
lzEndpoint address is a contract that doesn't return tokens

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: to != lzEndpoint allows transfer to endpoint; but OFT.send uses _burn, not transfer
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Tokens transferred directly to endpoint address are irrecoverable
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Should not allow direct ERC20 transfers to lzEndpoint; or remove this dead exemption

analysis_questions: Can the LayerZero endpoint contract return tokens sent via direct transfer?
Does the documentation clearly state users must use OFT.send() not transfer?
What is the lzEndpoint contract's behavior when receiving ERC20 tokens directly?
validation_requirements: Transfer TITN directly to lzEndpoint address via transfer()
Verify tokens are stuck at endpoint with no recovery
Confirm OFT.send uses _burn path not transfer

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: On Arbitrum, user sees that transfers to lzEndpoint are allowed. User calls titn.transfer(lzEndpoint, 1000e18) expecting bridging. Tokens are sent to the endpoint contract but no cross-chain message is initiated (that requires OFT.send). Tokens are permanently stuck at the endpoint address.
prerequisites: User mistakenly uses transfer instead of OFT.send

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Full loss of transferred token amount
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Users who attempt direct transfer to lzEndpoint
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: None — tokens are stuck at endpoint

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for direct transfer to lzEndpoint behavior

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Observable via token balance at lzEndpoint address

threats.title: No Minimum Output Protection Allows Near-Zero TITN for TGT Deposits Near End of Decay Period
threats.hypothesis_description: onTokenTransfer does not validate that quoteTitn returns a non-zero or minimum output amount. As timeSinceLaunch approaches 360 days, the linear decay formula produces progressively smaller TITN amounts. Users depositing TGT near the end of the 270-day decay period may receive negligible TITN (e.g., <1 token for significant TGT input) with no slippage protection or minimum output parameter.
threats.investigation_rationale: quoteTitn's decay formula: tgtAmount * TITN_ARB * remainingtime / (TGT_TO_EXCHANGE * 270 days). With 1 day remaining: output ≈ tgtAmount/900. For small TGT amounts, integer division truncation can result in 0. No require(titnOut > 0) exists.

proof_of_signal.pattern: titnOut = quoteTitn(amount) with no minimum output check; quoteTitn can return values approaching 0 due to integer division near 360-day boundary

preconditions: timeSinceLaunch is between 90 and 360 days (decay period)
User deposits TGT via transferAndCall
No off-chain UI warning about low output

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: claimableTitnPerUser[from] += titnOut without checking titnOut > minimumOutput
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Integer division truncation to 0 for small amounts near boundary; no minimum output parameter in onTokenTransfer

analysis_questions: What is the smallest TGT amount that results in 0 TITN at various time points?
Does the extraData parameter in onTokenTransfer serve any purpose (e.g., minOutput)?
Is precision loss accepted by the protocol for small amounts?
validation_requirements: Calculate titnOut for various TGT amounts at different time points
Identify threshold where output becomes effectively zero
Verify TGT is permanently locked when output is 0

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Near day 359, user deposits 100 TGT. quoteTitn returns ~0.11 TITN (or 0 due to truncation for smaller amounts). TGT is locked in contract, user gets negligible or zero TITN. The extraData parameter is unused, so no slippage protection is possible.
prerequisites: Deposit occurs late in the decay period
Amount is small enough relative to remaining time

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Loss of deposited TGT with negligible TITN return
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Users depositing TGT late in the merge period
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: None — no mechanism to return TGT

threats.test_coverage_gap: No boundary value tests for quoteTitn precision near end of decay

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: User can call quoteTitn view function before depositing

threats.title: withdrawRemainingTitn Perpetual Re-initialization DoS When Contract Balance Is Zero
threats.hypothesis_description: If the contract's TITN balance is exactly 0 when the first user calls withdrawRemainingTitn after 360 days (e.g., due to owner drain), remainingTitnAfter1Year is set to 0. The condition `if (remainingTitnAfter1Year == 0)` will re-trigger on every subsequent call, perpetually re-initializing remainingTitnAfter1Year=0 and initialTotalClaimable to the current totalTitnClaimable. If totalTitnClaimable > 0, the subtraction unclaimedTitn = 0 - initialTotalClaimable underflows and reverts, permanently blocking all claims.
threats.investigation_rationale: The initialization guard `if (remainingTitnAfter1Year == 0)` does not use a separate boolean flag. When balance is zero, it sets remainingTitnAfter1Year=0, which means the guard condition is always true on subsequent calls, creating an infinite re-initialization loop that always underflows.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (remainingTitnAfter1Year == 0) { remainingTitnAfter1Year = currentRemainingTitn; } — when currentRemainingTitn is 0, guard re-triggers every call

preconditions: titn.balanceOf(address(this)) == 0 at the time of first post-360-day call
totalTitnClaimable > 0 (at least one user has unclaimed TITN)
Owner has drained TITN or all TITN was claimed before 360 days

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Zero-value guard: remainingTitnAfter1Year initialized to 0 stays 0 when balance is 0
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Re-initialization loop followed by underflow on every call
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Should use a separate boolean `initialized` flag instead of checking remainingTitnAfter1Year == 0

analysis_questions: Under what realistic conditions is the contract balance 0 at 360 days?
Does owner withdrawal before 360 days drain all TITN?
Can users call claimTitn to empty the contract before 360 days?
validation_requirements: Drain contract TITN to 0 before 360 days
Call withdrawRemainingTitn after 360 days
Verify perpetual revert

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Owner withdraws all TITN before 360 days. After 360 days, user calls withdrawRemainingTitn. remainingTitnAfter1Year = 0, initialTotalClaimable = totalTitnClaimable > 0. unclaimedTitn = 0 - totalTitnClaimable → underflow revert. Every future call hits the same path because remainingTitnAfter1Year remains 0.
prerequisites: Contract TITN balance is 0 at time of first post-year call

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: All unclaimed TITN allocations permanently locked
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All users with positive claimableTitnPerUser at 360 days
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: None via contract — owner would need to manually refund

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for withdrawRemainingTitn when contract balance is 0

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by checking contract TITN balance at 360 days

threats.title: Owner Changing transferAllowedContract Breaks All MergeTgt Claim Operations on Arbitrum
threats.hypothesis_description: On Arbitrum (chainId 42161), MergeTgt is set as the transferAllowedContract on Titn. When MergeTgt calls titn.safeTransfer in claimTitn or withdrawRemainingTitn, the _validateTransfer function allows it because from == transferAllowedContract. If the owner changes transferAllowedContract to a different address (e.g., a staking contract), MergeTgt loses its exemption. All safeTransfer calls from MergeTgt will revert, permanently DoS-ing all TITN claims.
threats.investigation_rationale: Titn.sol allows only ONE transferAllowedContract at a time. The deployment sets this to MergeTgt. If owner changes it, there's no multi-address allowlist — MergeTgt is blocked. Since block.chainid == 42161 restricts ALL Arbitrum transfers, MergeTgt has no other bypass.
deployment_assumptions: Owner does not change transferAllowedContract away from MergeTgt while claims are pending
threats.config_evidence: arbitrumSetup.ts line: await titn.setTransferAllowedContract(deploymentMergeTgt.address) — sets MergeTgt as the single allowed contract

proof_of_signal.pattern: Single transferAllowedContract address; _validateTransfer checks from != transferAllowedContract

preconditions: Deployed on Arbitrum (chainId 42161)
Owner calls setTransferAllowedContract with address != MergeTgt
Users still have pending TITN claims in MergeTgt

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Only one transferAllowedContract at a time; changing it removes MergeTgt exemption
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: MergeTgt.claimTitn and withdrawRemainingTitn call titn.safeTransfer which triggers _validateTransfer
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No multi-address allowlist; no protection preventing change while claims are pending

analysis_questions: Would the owner have a legitimate reason to change transferAllowedContract during merge period?
Is there a staking contract that needs to be the transferAllowedContract simultaneously?
Could an address allowlist replace the single-address pattern?
validation_requirements: Set transferAllowedContract to a non-MergeTgt address
Attempt claimTitn — verify it reverts with BridgedTokensTransferLocked
Verify no workaround exists on Arbitrum

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Owner sets transferAllowedContract to a staking contract address. MergeTgt loses its transfer exemption on Arbitrum. Users calling claimTitn or withdrawRemainingTitn trigger titn.safeTransfer which reverts because MergeTgt is no longer the transferAllowedContract and block.chainid == 42161 restricts all transfers.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: All pending TITN claims become inaccessible
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All users with unclaimed TITN in MergeTgt
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Owner can restore by setting transferAllowedContract back to MergeTgt

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for interaction between transferAllowedContract changes and MergeTgt operations

threats.title: Owner Can Drain All User-Claimable TITN via Unrestricted withdraw Function
threats.hypothesis_description: The withdraw function allows the owner to transfer any amount of any ERC20 token from the MergeTgt contract, including TITN designated for user claims. There is no check against outstanding user balances (totalTitnClaimable), no restriction on withdrawing the TITN token, and no timelock. Owner can drain all TITN while users hold non-zero claimableTitnPerUser, causing all subsequent claimTitn and withdrawRemainingTitn calls to revert.
threats.investigation_rationale: withdraw(titn, titn.balanceOf(address(this))) drains everything. The contract's solvency invariant (balance >= totalTitnClaimable) is completely unprotected from owner actions. This is not a bug in logic but a missing safeguard — the withdraw function should restrict token withdrawal to amounts not committed to users.

proof_of_signal.pattern: function withdraw(IERC20 token, uint256 amount) external onlyOwner { token.safeTransfer(owner(), amount); } — no check against totalTitnClaimable

preconditions: Owner account is compromised or acts maliciously
Users have pending claimable TITN in the contract

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Unrestricted withdraw of any token including user-committed TITN
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: No guard like require(token != titn || amount <= balance - totalTitnClaimable)
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No restriction on withdrawing committed TITN; no timelock; no multi-sig

analysis_questions: Is the owner a multi-sig or EOA?
Should withdraw restrict TITN to surplus (balance - totalTitnClaimable)?
Is there a timelock contract wrapping the owner?
validation_requirements: Owner calls withdraw(titn, totalBalance)
Verify claimTitn reverts for users with positive claimable
Verify withdrawRemainingTitn also fails post-360 days

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Compromised owner calls withdraw(titn, titn.balanceOf(address(this))). All TITN drained to owner address. Users with claimableTitnPerUser > 0 call claimTitn → safeTransfer reverts (insufficient balance). After 360 days, withdrawRemainingTitn also reverts due to underflow. All user claims permanently blocked.
prerequisites: Owner key compromise or malicious owner

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Up to 173.7M TITN — complete protocol fund loss
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All users who deposited TGT and have pending TITN claims
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: None via contract — requires legal/social recovery

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test verifying withdraw restrictions on committed TITN

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Immediately visible via TITN balance change

threats.title: withdrawRemainingTitn Accounting Mismatch: totalTitnClaimed Includes Bonus but totalTitnClaimable Only Decremented by Base
threats.hypothesis_description: In withdrawRemainingTitn, totalTitnClaimed is incremented by titnOut (base + proportional bonus), but totalTitnClaimable is only decremented by claimableTitn (base amount only). The proportional bonus is never reflected in totalTitnClaimable. This creates an accounting divergence where totalTitnClaimed + totalTitnClaimable no longer accurately tracks the contract's obligation, and the invariant totalTitnClaimed + totalTitnClaimable <= TITN_ARB can be violated after bonus distributions.
threats.investigation_rationale: The totalTitnClaimed accumulator grows faster than totalTitnClaimable shrinks, creating inconsistency. While this may not directly cause fund loss in normal operation (since individual user claims use their own mapping), any off-chain system or future integration relying on these accumulators for solvency checks will get incorrect data.

proof_of_signal.pattern: totalTitnClaimed += titnOut; ... totalTitnClaimable -= claimableTitn; — titnOut includes bonus but claimableTitn does not

preconditions: At least one user calls withdrawRemainingTitn after 360 days
unclaimedTitn > 0 (there is bonus TITN to distribute)

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Asymmetric state update: totalTitnClaimed += full payout; totalTitnClaimable -= base only
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: totalTitnClaimed + totalTitnClaimable will exceed TITN_ARB after bonus distributions; accounting breaks for monitoring
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No special handling of bonus amount in global accumulators

analysis_questions: Does any on-chain logic depend on totalTitnClaimed or totalTitnClaimable for correctness?
Does the accounting mismatch affect individual user payouts?
Could this cause the last user's withdrawal to fail due to insufficient balance?
validation_requirements: Simulate multiple users calling withdrawRemainingTitn
Track totalTitnClaimed + totalTitnClaimable against TITN_ARB
Verify whether last user can still withdraw successfully

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Multiple users call withdrawRemainingTitn. Each adds (base + bonus) to totalTitnClaimed but only subtracts base from totalTitnClaimable. After all users claim, totalTitnClaimed = remainingTitnAfter1Year (correct actual outflow), but totalTitnClaimable = 0 and the sum totalTitnClaimed + totalTitnClaimable > TITN_ARB, breaking the conservation invariant for any monitoring system.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: No direct fund loss, but broken accounting may mislead integrations or monitoring

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test tracking global accumulator consistency through withdrawRemainingTitn

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Observable by comparing totalTitnClaimed + totalTitnClaimable vs TITN_ARB

threats.title: TwoWay LockedStatus Defined but Not Implemented — Missing Reverse Exchange Functionality
threats.hypothesis_description: The LockedStatus enum defines three states: Locked, OneWay, and TwoWay. However, the codebase treats OneWay and TwoWay identically — both simply allow TGT→TITN deposits in onTokenTransfer. No reverse exchange (TITN→TGT) function exists. If TwoWay was intended to enable bidirectional exchange, this is missing implementation. Users or governance expecting TwoWay mode to allow returning TGT have no recourse.
threats.investigation_rationale: onTokenTransfer checks `lockedStatus == LockedStatus.Locked` — it reverts only if Locked. Both OneWay and TwoWay pass the check identically. No code path exists for a TITN→TGT reverse swap, suggesting either incomplete implementation or misleading enum design.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (lockedStatus == LockedStatus.Locked || launchTime == 0) revert; — no distinction between OneWay and TwoWay

preconditions: Owner sets lockedStatus to TwoWay expecting bidirectional exchange
Users expect ability to reverse their TGT deposit

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: TwoWay enum value defined but functionally identical to OneWay in all code paths
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Missing reverse exchange function that TwoWay should enable
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No TITN→TGT swap function; no differentiation of OneWay vs TwoWay behavior

analysis_questions: Was TwoWay intended to enable reverse exchanges?
Is there documentation describing TwoWay behavior?
Is this dead code or a missing feature?
validation_requirements: Set lockedStatus to TwoWay
Verify no new functionality is enabled compared to OneWay
Search for any code that checks for TwoWay specifically

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Governance/owner sets TwoWay expecting users can return TITN for TGT. Users deposit TGT, then discover they cannot reverse the exchange. If TGT price rises relative to TITN, users are stuck with an unfavorable one-way conversion they expected to be reversible.
prerequisites: Expectation that TwoWay enables reverse exchange

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Users unable to reverse unfavorable exchanges
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Users who deposited TGT expecting TwoWay reversibility
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Only owner can withdraw TGT and manually return — no automated path
